24: A direct revelation: everyone who hears these words of mine and does them will be like a wise man who built his house on the rock. Jesus presents himself as the Rock — the authoritative foundation for a life that withstands final judgment. The shift from Moses's covenant (obey and live) to Jesus's covenant (hear my words and do them) is implicit but decisive: the words Jesus speaks carry the same covenant-binding authority as Torah, because he is the Torah-author become flesh, speaking the fulfillment of what the text was always pointing toward.
1: A revelation of God: "Judge not, that you be not judged" — Jesus presents the divine economy of judgment as the standard that governs his disciples' conduct. The one who will himself judge (Matthew 7:21-23; Matthew 25:31-46) is also the one who teaches mercy as the character of the kingdom. The standard "as you judge, so you will be judged" (Matthew 7:2) embeds the principle that the final judgment will mirror the conduct of the one judged — a theme fully revealed in the Last Judgment discourse (Matthew 25:31-46) where mercy shown to "the least of these" is mercy shown to Christ himself.
2: A revelation of God: the measure used in judgment will be applied to the judge. This principle anticipates the parables of the unmerciful servant (Matthew 18:21-35) and the talents (Matthew 25:14-30), where those who received mercy and hoarded it lose what they had. The Christological bearing: Christ himself embodies the ultimate mercy — receiving judgment in place of those who deserved it — which is the definitive answer to this principle rather than its circumvention.
3: A theme: the image of the speck and the log in the eye — the radical disproportion between perceived fault in the neighbor and actual fault in the self — recurs in the NT wisdom tradition (Romans 2:1-3; Matthew 14:10-13). Jesus is the one who sees perfectly, without the log; his judgment alone is unimpaired. The disciple's self-examination before judgment is a form of standing before the one who sees all things.
4: A theme: the rhetorical question "How can you say to your brother, 'Let me take the speck out of your eye,' when there is the log in your own eye?" exposes the absurdity of unreformed self-appointed correction. The eschatological resonance: the one who will judge at the last day (Christ) is also the one who has removed the full weight of sin from those he saves, qualifying him alone for the role of judge.
5: A revelation of God: the sequence — remove the log first, then you will see clearly to remove the speck — is the order of the kingdom: transformation precedes service. The Christological application is that the disciple's capacity to help the neighbor flows from having been healed by Christ, not from innate moral superiority. The "clear sight" promised after self-examination points to the renewed vision the Spirit gives (John 9:39-41).
6: A theme: do not give dogs what is holy, nor cast pearls before pigs. The wisdom saying draws on the OT distinction between clean and unclean animals as a metaphor for the discernment of receptivity. In Matthew's context, this anticipates the missionary instructions of Matthew 10 (shake off the dust, Matthew 10:14) and the parable of the sower (Matthew 13:3-9): the gospel seed is not withheld, but the teacher must discern contexts. The holy thing entrusted to the disciples is ultimately the message of the kingdom that Christ himself embodies.
7: A revelation of God: "Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and it will be opened to you" — the promise of response to persistent prayer. The one who gives is the heavenly Father (Matthew 7:11); the one who teaches this prayer is the Son who knows the Father perfectly (Matthew 11:27). The prayer that avails is ultimately the prayer that aligns with the Son's own will — "not as I will, but as you will" (Matthew 26:39). The invitation to ask is grounded in the character of the Father revealed in Christ.
8: A revelation of God: the universal promise — "everyone who asks receives, everyone who seeks finds, everyone who knocks it will be opened" — extends the invitation beyond the immediate circle. The inclusivity anticipates the Great Commission (Matthew 28:19): everyone who comes, from all nations, finds the door open. The passive verbs ("will be given," "will find," "will be opened") are divine passives — the Father acts in response to the disciples' asking.
9: A revelation of God: the analogical argument from human fatherhood to divine fatherhood — what father gives his son a stone when he asks for bread? The argument assumes that human parental love, flawed as it is, is a pale reflection of divine love. Jesus teaches the Father's character by analogy, and in so doing positions himself as the one who perfectly knows the Father and reveals him (Matthew 11:27; John 14:9). The bread/stone contrast anticipates the temptation narrative's stone-to-bread offer (Matthew 4:3) and the eucharistic bread.
10: A revelation of God: the fish/serpent pair in the analogical argument extends the Father's generosity to provision and protection. In context, the disciples are being sent as fishers of people (Matthew 4:19) into hostile territory (Matthew 10:16: "wise as serpents"); the Father who gives good things is also the one who protects and provides in the mission. Christ is the guarantee of this provision — he has gone before the disciples into every hostile situation.
11: A revelation of God: "If you, being evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father in heaven give good things to those who ask him?" — the a fortiori argument from human to divine. Luke 11:13 replaces "good things" with "the Holy Spirit," showing the Good Thing: the ultimate gift of the Father through the Son is the Spirit. The Christological bearing is direct: Jesus is the one who will ask the Father and the Father will give the Spirit (John 14:16).
12: A revelation of God: "So whatever you wish that others would do to you, do also to them, for this is the Law and the Prophets." The Golden Rule summarizes the whole Torah, prefacing the saying of fulfillment in Matthew 5:17 ("I came not to abolish but to fulfill"). Christ is the full embodiment of the Golden Rule: he does to others what they need, not what they deserve. The Law and Prophets finding their summary in the Golden Rule points to Christ as their fulfillment in person.
13: A direct teaching: "Enter by the narrow gate. For the gate is wide and the way is easy that leads to destruction, and those who enter by it are many." The narrow gate is Christ himself (John 10:9: "I am the door; if anyone enters by me, he will be saved"). The two-way motif has OT roots (Deuteronomy 30:15-19, the two ways; Psalms 1:6, the way of the righteous and the way of the wicked) and is fulfilled in the person of Christ as the one way (John 14:6).
14: A direct teaching: "For the gate is narrow and the way is hard that leads to life, and those who find it are few." The few who find it contrasts with the many on the broad road — not a fixed predestination of quantity but a statement about the counter-intuitive nature of the kingdom way. The "hard way" is discipleship-with-Christ, which requires taking up the cross (Matthew 16:24). Life (zōē) is the same word used in John 3:16 and 14:6 for the life Christ brings and is.
15: A revelation of God: "Beware of false prophets, who come to you in sheep's clothing but inwardly are ravenous wolves." The prophetic tradition in Israel was constantly threatened by false prophecy (Deuteronomy 13:1-5; Jeremiah 23:9-40; Ezekiel 13:1-23). Jesus positions himself as the true prophet and the standard by which false prophets are tested: his fruit (life, healing, truth, the cross) defines what genuine prophetic ministry looks like. The eschatological intensification: false Christs and prophets will increase as the end approaches (Matthew 24:11, 24).
16: A revelation of God: "You will recognize them by their fruits." The fruit test echoes Isaiah 5:1-7 (the vineyard that bore wild grapes instead of fruit) and anticipates the fig tree parables (Matthew 21:18-22; Matthew 21:33-46). The one who brings good fruit is Christ himself — the vine that produces through his branches (John 15:5). False prophets are known by fruit; Christ is known by his fruit, and his disciples by whether they abide in him.
17: A theme: "Every healthy tree bears good fruit, but the diseased tree bears bad fruit." The organic connection between the tree's nature and its fruit is Paul's argument in Galatians 5 (the fruit of the Spirit vs. the works of the flesh). The Christological application: the new humanity Christ inaugurates is a new tree — the vine of which he is the stock — producing fruit that is impossible for the diseased human nature unaided by grace.
18: A theme: "A healthy tree cannot bear bad fruit, nor can a diseased tree bear good fruit." The impossibility argument cuts both ways: neither Christ (the good tree) nor the unreformed human nature (the diseased tree) can produce fruit contrary to their nature. This anticipates the regeneration teaching of John 3 and the Spirit's role in transformation. Only union with Christ — the good tree — makes good fruit possible for the disciple.
19: A revelation of God: "Every tree that does not bear good fruit is cut down and thrown into the fire." The judgment image recurs throughout the Sermon (Matthew 5:22, 29-30; Matthew 7:23) and the parables (Matthew 13:40-42; Matthew 25:41). John the Baptist announced this judgment (Matthew 3:10: "Even now the axe is laid to the root of the trees"). Christ is both the one who bears the fire of judgment on behalf of the tree (the cross) and the one who executes final judgment (Matthew 25:31-46).
20: A revelation of God: "Thus you will recognize them by their fruits." The fruit test is the hermeneutical key to prophetic discernment throughout the OT: the prophet who leads toward faithfulness to YHWH and bears the marks of genuine commission is validated; the one who leads away is false. Jesus is the definitive validation criterion — teachers who lead toward him are confirmed by their fruit; those who lead away from him are exposed by theirs.
21: A direct teaching: "Not everyone who says to me, 'Lord, Lord,' will enter the kingdom of heaven, but the one who does the will of my Father who is in heaven." This verse asserts Christ's eschatological authority — he is the one who opens or bars entrance to the kingdom. The discernment between genuine and nominal faith is his prerogative. "The will of my Father" is ultimately revealed as trust in the Son whom the Father sent (John 6:40), making this a statement about the centrality of authentic relation to Christ.
22: A direct teaching: "On that day many will say to me, 'Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in your name, and cast out demons in your name, and do many mighty works in your name?'" — Christ is the final judge before whom all accounts are rendered. The use of his name in prophetic or miracle ministry does not automatically constitute relationship with him. The Christological weight: Christ knows his own (John 10:14); the final judgment is a disclosure of who truly knew him and was known by him, not merely a review of religious activity.
23: A direct teaching: "And then will I declare to them, 'I never knew you; depart from me, you workers of lawlessness.'" The pronouncement "I never knew you" is the most severe statement of exclusion in the Gospel and uniquely Christological: the standard for eschatological inclusion is not works, religious service, or even miracles performed in Christ's name, but genuine mutual knowledge — "I know my own and my own know me" (John 10:14). The echo of Psalms 6:8 ("Depart from me, all you workers of evil") places Christ in the position of YHWH who speaks that psalm.
25: A type: the wise man who built his house on the rock — "everyone who hears these words of mine and does them will be like a wise man who built his house on the rock." The rock foundation recurs at Matthew 16:18 (Peter and the church built on the rock of Christ's identity) and in 1 Corinthians 10:4 (the spiritual rock that followed Israel was Christ). The two houses — one on rock, one on sand — correspond to the two ways of Matthew 7:13-14. Christ as the rock is the Christological fulfillment of the rock of Horeb (Exodus 17:6) and the cornerstone of Psalms 118:22.
26: A theme: the foolish man who built on sand — "everyone who hears these words of mine and does not do them will be like a foolish man who built his house on the sand." The sand-builder hears but does not act; the Christological implication is that hearing Christ's words without obedience is the ultimate foolishness. The destruction of the sand-built house anticipates the judgment discourse of Matthew 24-25 and the parable of the ten virgins — the crisis that separates those who were genuinely prepared from those who merely appeared to be.
27: A revelation of God: "And the rain fell, and the floods came, and the winds blew and beat against that house, and it fell, and great was the fall of it." The storm that destroys the sand-built house is a judgment image with OT roots (Ezekiel 13:10-16: the wall built on sand that cannot withstand the storm of divine judgment). The eschatological storm is the judgment Jesus announces as coming; the only security against it is the rock-foundation of doing his words. Christ is both the rock and the standard of judgment.
28: A direct teaching: "And when Jesus finished these sayings, the crowds were astonished at his teaching." The closing formula of the Sermon on the Mount — the crowds' astonishment — echoes the people's response at Sinai. Moses descended with the Law given by God; Jesus taught on the mountain as the one who is God. The astonishment (exeplēssonto) is the response to the divine presence speaking through human form — the same word used for responses to Jesus' authority throughout the Gospel (Matthew 13:54; Matthew 19:25; Matthew 22:33).
29: A direct teaching: "For he was teaching them as one who had authority, and not as their scribes." The scribal teaching method (kekah shemah — "I have heard it said") quoted tradition and precedent; Jesus taught from his own authority (exousia). This is the Christological claim embedded in the form of his teaching: the one who gave the Torah at Sinai is now its authoritative interpreter and fulfiller. The Sermon's "But I say to you" antitheses made explicit what this verse states as the crowd's perception.
